subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 6, young mathematicians! In this unit you will explore the fascinating world of 2D shapes – the flat shapes that are all around you every single day. A 2D shape (short for two-dimensional) is a flat shape that has only length and width, but no thickness. When Amina looks at the tiles on the masjid floor, when Yusuf notices the triangular warning sign on the road to Hargeisa, or when Halima admires the six-sided cells of a honeycomb, they are all looking at 2D shapes.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Primary Mathematics 0096 — Stage 4 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: This unit is designed for self-paced learning. That means you move at your own speed. Read each explanation slowly, draw every shape yourself on paper, and only move on when you feel ready. Your AI tutor is available day and night to draw a shape a new way, give you extra practice, or check your answers. Your teacher will also meet with you about twice a week to help with anything that feels tricky.

Why does the study of shapes matter so much? Geometry is the mathematics of the space around you. Builders in Mogadishu use shapes to design strong, straight walls. Artists use tessellation and symmetry to create beautiful patterns on cloth and pottery. Farmers near Kismaayo work out the perimeter of a field to know how much fencing to buy. Even the patches on a football are shapes – pentagons and hexagons stitched together. When you understand shapes, you begin to see the hidden geometry in everything, from a window frame to a woven mat.

As you work, keep a pencil, a ruler and some paper beside you. Geometry is not a subject you can learn only by reading – you must draw it. Trace around shapes, fold paper to find symmetry, and cut out shapes to see which ones tessellate. If you draw a shape wrongly, that is not a failure; it simply shows you exactly what to practise next. Let us begin our journey into the world of flat shapes.
Identify and name a variety of 2D shapes, including triangles, quadrilaterals, pentagons, hexagons and octagons.
Describe the properties of 2D shapes using sides, vertices and angles.
Tell the difference between regular and irregular polygons.
Classify triangles (equilateral, isosceles, scalene, right-angled) and quadrilaterals (square, rectangle, rhombus, parallelogram, trapezium, kite).
Recognise and create tessellation patterns, and explain why some shapes tessellate.
Find and draw lines of symmetry in 2D shapes.
Recognise parallel and perpendicular lines in shapes.

concepts.title: Naming 2D Shapes
concepts.explanation: Let us begin by naming the shapes you will meet in this unit. A polygon is a closed flat shape made entirely of straight sides – no curves and no gaps. Polygons are named by their number of sides. Here are the ones you need to know well: Triangle – a closed shape with 3 straight sides and 3 vertices.
concepts.example: Quadrilateral – any shape with 4 straight sides and 4 vertices (this includes squares, rectangles and more).

concepts.title: Sides, Vertices and Angles
concepts.explanation: Every polygon can be described using three key properties: its sides, its vertices and its angles. Learning to describe a shape by these properties is like learning to describe a person by their height, their name and their voice – the properties tell you exactly which shape you are dealing with. The sides are the straight lines that make up the shape. The vertices (one corner is called a vertex) are the points where two sides meet. Here is a very important pattern: in any polygon, the number of sides always…
concepts.example: An angle is the amount of turn between two sides that meet at a vertex. Angles are measured in degrees, written with a small circle like this: 90°. There are four kinds of angle you should recognise:

concepts.title: Regular and Irregular Polygons
concepts.explanation: Polygons come in two important kinds: regular and irregular. This is one of the most useful ideas in the whole unit, so let us understand it carefully. A regular polygon has all its sides equal in length AND all its angles equal in measure. Both conditions must be true. A square is the perfect example of a regular polygon: all four sides are the same length and all four angles are 90°. An equilateral triangle (three equal sides, three equal angles) is a regular triangle. A regular pentagon has five equal sides and…
concepts.example: An irregular polygon has sides or angles that are not all equal. A rectangle that is not a square is a good example: its angles are all 90°, but its sides are not all the same length (two long sides and two short…

concepts.title: Classifying Triangles
concepts.explanation: The triangle is the simplest polygon, but it comes in several different types. We can classify (sort) triangles in two ways: by their sides and by their angles. A Year 4 mathematician should be able to name each type. An equilateral triangle has all 3 sides equal and all 3 angles equal (each 60°). It is a regular triangle.
concepts.example: An isosceles triangle has exactly 2 sides equal and the two angles at its base equal.

concepts.title: Classifying Quadrilaterals
concepts.explanation: A quadrilateral is any polygon with 4 straight sides and 4 vertices. There are several special quadrilaterals, each with its own properties. Let us meet the whole family. 4 equal sides; 4 right angles (90°); opposite sides parallel
concepts.example: Opposite sides equal; 4 right angles; opposite sides parallel

concepts.title: Tessellation
concepts.explanation: Tessellation happens when copies of a shape fit together perfectly to cover a flat surface, with no gaps and no overlaps. It is like a puzzle where every piece fits exactly. You see tessellation in the floor tiles of the masjid, in the cells of a honeycomb, and in many woven mats and patterned cloths. Some shapes tessellate easily on their own: squares, rectangles, triangles and regular hexagons all tile a surface perfectly. Other shapes will not tessellate by themselves: circles leave curved gaps, and regular…
concepts.example: Why do some shapes tessellate while others do not? The secret is in the angles that meet at a point. For shapes to fit around a point with no gaps, the angles meeting there must add up to exactly 360° – a full turn. Let…

methods.title: How to name and describe a polygon
methods.example: A closed shape has 6 straight sides. Name it, and state how many vertices it has.
steps: Count the number of straight sides.
Match the count to the name: 3 = triangle, 4 = quadrilateral, 5 = pentagon, 6 = hexagon, 8 = octagon.
Check the sides and angles: if all equal, it is regular; if not, irregular.
Look for parallel sides, perpendicular sides and lines of symmetry.

methods.title: How to find a missing angle
methods.example: A closed shape has 6 straight sides. Name it, and state how many vertices it has.
steps: For a triangle, start from 180 degrees; for a quadrilateral, start from 360 degrees.
Add up the angles you already know.
Subtract that total from 180 (or 360) to find the missing angle.

methods.title: How to test for tessellation
methods.example: A triangle has angles of 70° and 60°. What is the third angle?
steps: Find the size of one interior angle of the shape.
Divide 360 by that angle.
If the answer is a whole number, the shape tessellates; if not, it does not.

methods.title: Will this shape tessellate?
methods.example: A triangle has angles of 70° and 60°. What is the third angle?
steps: Solution: The three angles of any triangle add up to 180°.
So the third angle = 180° − 70° − 60° = 50°.
Because all three angles are less than 90°, this is an acute triangle.
Worked Example 4: Will this shape tessellate?

methods.title: Lines of symmetry
methods.example: How many lines of symmetry does a square have, and how many does a rectangle have?
steps: Solution: A square has 4 lines of symmetry (2 through opposite sides, 2 through opposite corners).
A rectangle has only 2 lines of symmetry (through the midpoints of opposite sides).
The rectangle's diagonals are not lines of symmetry, because folding along them does not make the halves match.

methods.title: Classify a quadrilateral
methods.example: A shape has 4 equal sides but its angles are not all 90°. What is it, and is it regular?
steps: Solution: A quadrilateral with 4 equal sides but no right angles is a rhombus.
It is irregular, because although its sides are all equal, its angles are not all equal.
(If the angles were all 90°, it would be a square.)

workedExamples.title: Name the shape and count its properties
workedExamples.prompt: A closed shape has 6 straight sides. Name it, and state how many vertices it has.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: A polygon with 6 sides is a hexagon. Because the number of vertices always equals the number of sides, it has 6 vertices. If all its sides and angles are equal, it is a *regular* hexagon. Worked Example 2: Regular or irregular? Is a rectangle (with length 8 cm and width 4 cm) a regular or an irregular polygon? Explain. Solution: It is irregular. To be regular a shape needs equal sides AND equal angles. This rectangle has all angles equal to 90°, but its sides are not all equal (two are 8 cm and two are 4…

workedExamples.title: Regular or irregular?
workedExamples.prompt: A closed shape has 6 straight sides. Name it, and state how many vertices it has.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: A polygon with 6 sides is a hexagon. Because the number of vertices always equals the number of sides, it has 6 vertices. If all its sides and angles are equal, it is a *regular* hexagon. Worked Example 2: Regular or irregular? Is a rectangle (with length 8 cm and width 4 cm) a regular or an irregular polygon? Explain. Solution: It is irregular. To be regular a shape needs equal sides AND equal angles. This rectangle has all angles equal to 90°, but its sides are not all equal (two are 8 cm and two are 4…

workedExamples.title: Find the missing angle in a triangle
workedExamples.prompt: A triangle has angles of 70° and 60°. What is the third angle?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The three angles of any triangle add up to 180°. So the third angle = 180° − 70° − 60° = 50°. Because all three angles are less than 90°, this is an acute triangle. Worked Example 4: Will this shape tessellate? Each angle of a regular hexagon is 120°. Will regular hexagons tessellate? How many meet at each point? Solution: For tessellation the angles at a point must total 360°. Since 360° ÷ 120° = 3, exactly 3 hexagons meet at each point (120° + 120° + 120° = 360°). So yes, regular hexagons tessellate…

workedExamples.title: Will this shape tessellate?
workedExamples.prompt: A triangle has angles of 70° and 60°. What is the third angle?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The three angles of any triangle add up to 180°. So the third angle = 180° − 70° − 60° = 50°. Because all three angles are less than 90°, this is an acute triangle. Worked Example 4: Will this shape tessellate? Each angle of a regular hexagon is 120°. Will regular hexagons tessellate? How many meet at each point? Solution: For tessellation the angles at a point must total 360°. Since 360° ÷ 120° = 3, exactly 3 hexagons meet at each point (120° + 120° + 120° = 360°). So yes, regular hexagons tessellate…

workedExamples.title: Lines of symmetry
workedExamples.prompt: How many lines of symmetry does a square have, and how many does a rectangle have?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: A square has 4 lines of symmetry (2 through opposite sides, 2 through opposite corners). A rectangle has only 2 lines of symmetry (through the midpoints of opposite sides). The rectangle's diagonals are not lines of symmetry, because folding along them does not make the halves match.

workedExamples.title: Classify a quadrilateral
workedExamples.prompt: A shape has 4 equal sides but its angles are not all 90°. What is it, and is it regular?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: A quadrilateral with 4 equal sides but no right angles is a rhombus. It is irregular, because although its sides are all equal, its angles are not all equal. (If the angles were all 90°, it would be a square.)

workedExamples.title: Perimeter of a shape
workedExamples.prompt: A regular pentagon has sides of 8 cm each. What is its perimeter?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The perimeter is the total distance around the shape. A regular pentagon has 5 equal sides, so perimeter = 5 × 8 cm = 40 cm.

workedExamples.title: Angles meeting at a point
workedExamples.prompt: Amina wants to tile a floor with a shape whose angle is 90°. How many will fit around each point, and will they tessellate?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: 360° ÷ 90° = 4, so 4 shapes meet at each point (4 right angles make a full turn). Because they fit exactly with no gap, the shape tessellates. A 90° angle belongs to a square, and squares are famous for tessellating.

workedExamples.title: State whether each will tessellate by itself
workedExamples.prompt: State whether each will tessellate by itself: a) square b) circle c) regular hexagon d) regular pentagon.
workedExamples.solution: a) yes b) no c) yes d) no.

workedExamples.title: Calculate the perimeter
workedExamples.prompt: Calculate the perimeter: a) a square with side 7 cm b) a regular hexagon with side 6 cm.

workedExamples.title: Is each shape regular or irregular
workedExamples.prompt: Is each shape regular or irregular? a) a rectangle 10 cm by 4 cm b) an equilateral triangle c) a rhombus.
workedExamples.solution: a) irregular b) regular c) irregular.

workedExamples.title: Practice 12
workedExamples.prompt: Each angle of an equilateral triangle is 60°. How many triangles meet at a point when they tessellate? Show your working.

activities.title: Shape Hunt Around the Home
activities.materials: paper, a pencil, and your sharp eyes.
steps: Walk around your home and find at least ten flat shapes – windows, tiles, plates, signs, books, mats.
Draw each object and write the name of its 2D shape beside it.
For each shape, count and write down the number of sides and vertices.
Sort your shapes into two groups: those with straight sides (polygons) and the circle (not a polygon).
Which shape did you find most often around your home? Many people find rectangles everywhere!

activities.title: Fold to Find Symmetry
steps: Cut out a paper square, a rectangle, an equilateral triangle and a circle.
Fold each shape carefully to find every line where the two halves match exactly.
Draw a line along each fold and count the lines of symmetry.
Check your results: square = 4, rectangle = 2, equilateral triangle = 3, circle = many. Were you right?
Folding is the surest way to prove a line of symmetry – if the halves do not match, it is not a line of symmetry.

activities.title: Tessellation Tiles
steps: Cut out several identical squares, then several identical equilateral triangles, then several regular hexagons.
Try to arrange each shape to cover a flat surface with no gaps and no overlaps.
Now cut out several regular pentagons and try the same – what goes wrong?
Colour your best tessellating pattern to make a piece of tile art, just like the patterns on a masjid floor.
🔍 Investigation For each shape that tessellates, count how many shapes meet at one point. Add up the angles at that point. Do they always make 360 degrees, a full turn?

activities.title: The Quadrilateral Family Game
steps: Make six cards, one for each quadrilateral: square, rectangle, rhombus, parallelogram, trapezium, kite.
On the back of each card, write two of its properties (for example, '4 equal sides' or 'one pair of parallel sides').
Shuffle the cards and have a family member read a property while you name the shape.
Then play the family hierarchy game: can you explain why a square belongs in the rectangle group AND the rhombus group?

activities.title: Angle Detective
activities.materials: the corner of a piece of paper (a perfect right angle) and objects around the home.
steps: Hold the paper corner against the corners of shapes you find – a table, a window, a tile, a book.
Decide whether each corner is a right angle (fits exactly), acute (smaller) or obtuse (wider).
Find three right angles, two acute angles and two obtuse angles.
Look for perpendicular lines (two lines meeting at a right angle) and parallel lines (lines that stay the same distance apart).

activities.title: Build a Playground Design (Investigation)
activities.materials: a large sheet of paper, a ruler, and colouring pencils.
steps: Design a playground using only 2D shapes. Include at least five different shapes.
Add at least one tessellating pattern (for a floor or wall) and at least one symmetrical element.
Label every shape with its name and write one property beside it.
Write a short sentence explaining why you chose each shape for its purpose.

After your activities, talk or write about: Which activity helped you understand shapes best? Which shape surprised you when you tested it for symmetry or tessellation? What would you like more practice with? Share your answers with your teacher at your next session.

rules.title: Sides and Vertices Rule
rules.text: The number of sides always equals the number of vertices. A side is an edge (a line). A vertex is a corner (a point).

rules.title: Regular Polygon Rule
rules.text: A regular polygon has ALL sides equal AND ALL angles equal. Equal sides alone is not enough (a rhombus has equal sides but is irregular).

rules.title: Key rule 3
rules.text: Angle Sum Rules The angles inside any triangle add up to 180 degrees. The angles inside any quadrilateral add up to 360 degrees.

rules.title: Tessellation Rule
rules.text: A shape tessellates if its angle divides evenly into 360 degrees. Squares (90), triangles (60) and regular hexagons (120) tessellate.

terms: A closed shape made of straight sides
A corner where two sides meet
All sides and angles equal / not all equal
Shapes fitting with no gaps or overlaps
A fold line where two halves match
Never meet / meet at a right angle
The distance all the way around a shape
commonMistakes: A side is a line; a vertex is a point
A regular shape needs equal angles too
A rectangle has only 2 lines of symmetry
Rhombus, trapezium and kite are quadrilaterals too

realProblems.prompt: A rectangular garden in Mombasa is 8 metres long and 6 metres wide. How much fencing is needed to go all the way around the perimeter?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: A square floor tile has sides of 20 cm. Square tiles are laid so they meet at points. Explain, using angles, why they fit with no gaps.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: A mosaic artist uses equilateral triangles to make a pattern. If 6 triangles meet at each point, what is the total of the angles there, and why does this make the pattern work?
realProblems.answer: Each tile angle is 90°, and 4 × 90° = 360°, a full turn, so four tiles fit around each point with no gap.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Each tile angle is 90°, and 4 × 90° = 360°, a full turn, so four tiles fit around each point with no gap.

realProblems.prompt: A hexagonal garden bed at Nuur Academy has 6 equal sides of 3 metres each. What is the perimeter, and how many metres of edging are needed?
realProblems.answer: 6 × 60° = 360°; because the angles make a full turn with no gap or overlap, the triangles tessellate.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 6 × 60° = 360°; because the angles make a full turn with no gap or overlap, the triangles tessellate.

realProblems.prompt: Khadija designs a logo that must have exactly 3 lines of symmetry. Which regular polygon should she use as its base? Explain.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: Each angle of an equilateral triangle is 60°. How many triangles meet at a point when they tessellate? Show your working.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Each angle of an equilateral triangle is 60°. How many triangles meet at a point when they tessellate? Show your working.
A quadrilateral has three angles of 90°, 90° and 120°. Find the fourth angle. (Angles in a quadrilateral add to 360°.)
Explain why a rectangle has only 2 lines of symmetry while a square has 4.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: A square's diagonals are also fold lines because all sides are equal, giving 4; a rectangle's diagonals do not make the halves match, so only the 2 lines through the side midpoints work.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Sort these shapes into 'has parallel sides' and 'has no parallel sides': square, scalene triangle, parallelogram, equilateral triangle, trapezium.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Parallel sides: square, parallelogram, trapezium. No parallel sides: scalene triangle, equilateral triangle.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain the key idea in Classifying Quadrilaterals.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: A quadrilateral is any polygon with 4 straight sides and 4 vertices. There are several special quadrilaterals, each with its own properties. Let us meet the whole family. 4 equal sides; 4 right angles (90°); opposite sides parallel

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain the key idea in Tessellation.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Tessellation happens when copies of a shape fit together perfectly to cover a flat surface, with no gaps and no overlaps. It is like a puzzle where every piece fits exactly. You see tessellation in the floor tiles of the masjid, in the cells of a honeycomb, and in many woven mats and patterned cloths. Some shapes tessellate easily on their own: squares, rectangles, triangles and regular hexagons all tile a surface perfectly. Other shapes will not tessellate by themselves: circles leave curved gaps, and regular…

selfAssessment: I can this unit is designed for self-paced learning. That means you move at your own speed. Read each explanation slowly, draw every shape yourself on paper, and only move on when you feel ready. Your AI tutor is available day and night to draw a shape a new way, give you extra practice, or check your answers. Your teacher will also meet with you about twice a week to help with anything that feels tricky.

I can why does the study of shapes matter so much? Geometry is the mathematics of the space around you. Builders in Mogadishu use shapes to design strong, straight walls. Artists use tessellation and symmetry to create beautiful patterns on cloth and pottery. Farmers near Kismaayo work out the perimeter of a field to know how much fencing to buy. Even the patches on a football are shapes – pentagons and hexagons stitched together. When you understand shapes, you begin to see the hidden geometry in everything, from a window frame to a woven mat.

I can as you work, keep a pencil, a ruler and some paper beside you. Geometry is not a subject you can learn only by reading – you must draw it. Trace around shapes, fold paper to find symmetry, and cut out shapes to see which ones tessellate. If you draw a shape wrongly, that is not a failure; it simply shows you exactly what to practise next. Let us begin our journey into the world of flat shapes.
I can identify and name a variety of 2D shapes, including triangles, quadrilaterals, pentagons, hexagons and octagons.
I can describe the properties of 2D shapes using sides, vertices and angles.
I can tell the difference between regular and irregular polygons.
I can classify triangles (equilateral, isosceles, scalene, right-angled) and quadrilaterals (square, rectangle, rhombus, parallelogram, trapezium, kite).
I can recognise and create tessellation patterns, and explain why some shapes tessellate.
